data.name: Manuel Neuer
data.description: Without doubt the most talked-about selection in the World Cup squad. As recently as the spring, there had been no sign that Neuer, who had retired after Euro 2024, would make a return to the national team. Then, in early April, the 40-year-old put in a masterclass against Real Madrid in the first leg of Bayern’s Champions League quarter-final. He was man of the match, preserving a 2-1 win with saves and composure, and driving Kylian Mbappé and Vinícius Júnior to despair. Just like the old days. Even though he made a mistake in the second leg, hardly anyone doubts that Neuer is a better goalkeeper than Oliver Baumann, who turned 36 at the start of June. “Everyone knows what aura and quality Manu has, what he gives a team,” Julian Nagelsmann said. Whether Nagelsmann’s last-minute decision to recall Neuer (and demote Baumann from first-choice) has any effect on the players’ trust in the coach will only become clear in the coming weeks.

data.name: Antonio Rüdiger
data.description: The most-criticised German international of them all at the moment and Julian Nagelsmann has admitted that Rüdiger is a polarising figure. That has little to do with his performances, though, and much more with his behaviour in certain instances. There was the throat-slitting gesture towards Atlético Madrid’s fans, for which he was fined, and the tantrum after the 2025 Copa del Rey final against Barcelona when he tried to throw ice at the referee and called him, among other things, a “freak” (“Missgeburt” in German). He was suspended for six games. Add to that the challenge on Getafe’s Diego Rico in March and there is a pattern emerging. “When you’re under that much criticism as an international, then you start thinking,” Rüdiger later told the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung. He knows he has had incidents “that were clearly over the line” and admits he has not lived up to his responsibilities. “I don’t want to be a source of unrest, I want to provide stability and security,” he says. Remarkably, in more than 80 games for Germany he has never been sent off. For Real, after some injury problems, he was a fixture in the team towards the end of the season but, for Germany going into the World Cup, Tah and Schlotterbeck were ahead of him in the pecking order.

data.name: Waldemar Anton
data.description: “The defensive tower”, as he is known in the German media, is very popular – apart from in the Stuttgart metropolitan region. VfB fans have not forgiven him for moving to Dortmund after Euro 2024, even though he had extended his contract at Stuttgart shortly beforehand. “I originally had no intention of changing clubs – but then Borussia Dortmund came in. A top club that had just reached the Champions League final,” he said. He plays solidly, unspectacularly, and almost always well. He also dares to play a risky pass. “Waldemar Anton is an extremely reliable defender with a big heart,” Nagelsmann once said. He was born in Uzbekistan as Wladimir Anton but when his parents moved to Germany in 1998, his first name was changed to Waldemar. Short form: Wowa.

data.name: Jonathan Tah
data.description: Defensive leader at Bayern and defensive leader of the national team. Finally. At 19, Tah was named Germany’s best young footballer but, as time went on, many gave up on the idea that this huge defensive talent would ever become an important international. “There were injuries, dips in form, tough moments. People expected me to take on responsibility early. Maybe I put too much pressure on myself,” he told Die Zeit two years ago. Under Xabi Alonso at Leverkusen, however, Tah became the best centre-back in the Bundesliga: strong in duels, good in the air, hard to dribble past and with few mistakes. Alonso may, in fact, have saved Tah’s career. On Instagram, the 30-year-old describes himself as “dedicated, ambitious and open-minded individual, striving for the best version of himself”. That’s worked out pretty well in recent years. Tah will play his first World Cup match in North America.

data.name: Aleksandar Pavlović
data.description: The son of a German mother and a Serb father is a Munich native and has been wearing the Bayern shirt since he was seven. That is a good starting place to become a club legend and ever since Thomas Tuchel promoted him to the first team three years ago, he has been part of Bayern’s starting XI. These days he is also a starter for the national team. His role model? Sergio Busquets. “Busquets was very strong both with and without the ball,” Pavlovic once told the club magazine 51.”He was always available for a pass and always had an eye on his surroundings. On top of that, he was very solid defensively and covered a lot of ground.” He also admires Rodri. Pavlovic clearly sees himself as a No 6 but to become a world-class player in that position, needs to improve defensively.

data.name: Joshua Kimmich
data.description: It is a pity there are not two Joshua Kimmichs: one for central midfield, one for defence. Then the endless debate about which position he is more suited to could be avoided. For Bayern’s manager, Vincent Kompany, he is the first-choice No 6. Under Julian Nagelsmann he was first at right-back, then briefly returned to midfield, and now is meant to do everything at once as a defensive playmaker. In buildup play, he moves from right-back into the centre, which can be risky if he loses the ball. Luckily enough, he does not lose the ball that often. Recently moved past Jürgen Kohler and into the top 10 of Germany players with most appearances. With Bayern he has won the Champions League and 10 Bundesliga titles, but nothing yet with the national team. After the group-stage exit in Qatar, he spoke with tears in his eyes of the most difficult day of his career: “I’m a bit afraid of falling into a hole.” He has certainly worked his way out of it and, as captain, main motivator and “shirt-tucked-in” advocate, enjoys great respect in Germany.

data.name: Kai Havertz
data.description: The Arsenal forward loves donkeys. Really loves them. “It started when I was young, I somehow developed a love for them. The guys know about it too, and some of them joke about it,” he once confessed. “In general, a donkey is a great animal.” Arrives at the World Cup after another injury-interrupted campaign, having been playing regularly only in the spring. This intelligent striker is comfortable in many positions. Nagelsmann may use him as a centre-forward, but values his versatility. Scored the winning goal in the 2021 Champions League final for Chelsea against Manchester City but, despite making his Germany debut in 2018, he has not reached 60 caps yet.

data.name: Leon Goretzka
data.description: The 31-year-old Bayern midfielder has arguably the most turbulent on-off relationship with Julian Nagelsmann in German football. In Munich, they once formed a powerhouse duo and even as the Germany coach Nagelsmann initially selected the player, before surprisingly dropping him for Euro 2024 with the all-powerful Toni Kroos returning to the squad. Then peace talks were held. “A discussion was necessary; it was the basis for further cooperation,” Goretzka told Die Zeit. The therapy – probably candlelit and with a tactics board – worked. Goretzka is no longer a starter at Bayern and is set to leave the club at the end of the season, so to achieve anything with Germany in the summer, he needs to rediscover his true strength: scoring goals.

data.name: Jamie Leweling
data.description: Leweling almost did not play for Germany at all. His father is from Ghana and the country’s FA approached Leweling early on, but the youngster politely declined. “Because I’m German,” he said after making his Nationalmannschaft debut in 2024. “Of course I have roots there but I grew up here in Germany and that makes me German.” Born in Nuremberg, he has Bundesliga experience with Greuther Fürth, Union Berlin and Stuttgart. He arrives at the World Cup on the back of his best season ever. Known as a player who works extremely hard and someone who always does extra sessions, he is quick and has decided some Bundesliga games almost single-handed. Scored the only goal of the game on his international debut against the Netherlands.

data.name: Jamal Musiala
data.description: At 17, he scored in the Champions League and, at 19, was one of few Germany players to come out of the 2022 World Cup with his reputation intact, or even improved. The three goals he scored at Euro 2024 made him an international star. But then, at the 2025 Club World Cup, it all came to a shuddering halt with a gruesome injury suffered in a collision with Gianluigi Donnarumma. He was out for six months as Germany qualified for the World Cup and Bayern got off to a near-perfect start. He is not quite the same player he was before the injury but started and played well in the incredible 5-4 Champions League defeat at Paris Saint-Germain in April. To protect him, Nagelsmann left Musiala out of the friendlies in March amid some uncertainty over whether he should go to the World Cup. “If I feel that something isn’t right in my game,” says Oliver Kahn, “then I have to work on myself until I’m ready again.” Musiala, who earns more at Bayern than the England captain Harry Kane, must be careful not to be overtaken by Lennart Karl, who is five years younger.

data.name: Nick Woltemade
data.description: Woltemade’s rise has been rapid. Within a year he went from being a squad player at Stuttgart to becoming a €70m Premier League signing and a Germany international. His unorthodox style – at 1.98m (6ft 6in) tall, somewhat awkward – quickly made him a fan favourite. “Sometimes I lie in bed at night and think: 'Wow, this is all so crazy,'” he told Zeit Campus. In Newcastle, he lives in a shared apartment with his best friend. “We have breakfast together, chill in the evening, and watch football when I’m not playing.” After a promising start at Newcastle, however, Woltemade has lost his starting place and watched the Champions League tie against Barcelona from the bench. Still, he seems to be in favour with Julian Nagelsmann and scored four goals in World Cup qualifying.

data.name: Oliver Baumann
data.description: Germany’s No 2 – who until a few weeks ago was still No 1 – is known above all for being fairly unknown, even though he has made more than 500 Bundesliga appearances. That is partly because he played the majority of those matches for Hoffenheim. It is also because Baumann has only been an international goalkeeper for about 18 months. He took over the spot from the injury-plagued Marc-André ter Stegen. Baumann is dependable, and he proved that during a difficult World Cup qualifying campaign. He told Zeit: “I believe that one of my greatest strengths is consistency.” Baumann is unlike many of his predecessors, who were the sort of figures you had to fear (at least on the pitch): Toni Schumacher, Oliver Kahn, “Mad” Jens Lehmann. By contrast, Baumann is someone you could trust to, say, look after your pets. “I’m not a fan of big statements,” he said. “I think what should count are my actions on the pitch.”

data.name: Pascal Groß
data.description: He returned “home” in January – not to Mannheim, where he was born, but to the English seaside town of Brighton. He played there for seven years before moving to Dortmund, his first major European club. In the end, however, he wasn’t able to make an impact with the Bundesliga side and made the switch back. Once seen as a potential successor to Toni Kroos, Gross has not quite lived up to that.  A curious fact: he suffers from a grass allergy – quite inconvenient for a footballer. To avoid getting rashes, he tends to play in long-sleeve shirts. “I actually train in a base layer for my legs as well, no matter the weather,” he says. For this World Cup, it could get very hot for the midfielder.

data.name: Maximilian Beier
data.description: The only player to have been born in the east of the country. Beier is a big darts fan. When he scores, he sometimes celebrates by throwing an imaginary dart. What Beier especially admires is the composure of darts players such as Luke Littler. “I find it hard to imagine what that tunnel must be like when he is in it, that absolute focus,” he once told Kicker. Beier is light-footed, quick, carefree and capable of a flash of inspiration. The Dortmund forward thrives on intelligent movement off the ball, rather than one-on-one dribbling. At the World Cup he is unlikely to be more than an impact substitute, but he will certainly not cause any trouble because of that. When announcing the squad, Julian Nagelsmann praised Beier’s diligence. In a FAZ questionnaire, Beier said that the most important coach of his career had been Patrick Schrade, one of the coaches at the youth academy boarding school of Energie Cottbus.

data.name: Nico Schlotterbeck
data.description: The kind of defender many German football fans love: he always throws himself fully into challenges, is strong in the air, loves a sliding tackle and celebrates after winning duels. Playing against Schlotterbeck hurts. He also scores goals every now and then, after which he shows the crowd his impressive biceps. He can sometimes work himself into a frenzy and then is not always in control, becoming a security risk in his own penalty area. It would perhaps help if he took a leaf out of Fabio Cannavaro’s book of clever defending; the Italian was his role model after all. The only left-footer among Germany’s centre-backs, he was ruled out of the tournament after suffering an ankle ligament injury against Côte d'Ivoire.

data.name: Angelo Stiller
data.description: Was initially not included in the squad for the March friendlies, which Julian Nagelsmann said would give a clear indication of who was going to the World Cup. However, after injuries to Aleksandar Pavlovic and Felix Nmecha, he was not only called up, but started both wins against Switzerland and Ghana. Strange? Not in the coach’s world. “Stiller is an outstandingly good footballer with enormous potential and very consistent performances,” Nagelsmann says. Pavlovic goes into the World Cup as the favourite to start at No 6, but who knows what will happen.

data.name: Florian Wirtz
data.description: In Germany, Wirtz is known as the player who made Bayer Leverkusen’s miraculous league and cup double in 2024 possible, ending Bayern Munich’s longstanding dominance. He has also shone for Germany, including a sensational performance in the 4-3 victory against Switzerland in March, but his major breakthrough is yet to come. Has had an up-and-down first season at Anfield after his £100m transfer last summer with his now-departed manager, Arne Slot, saying things will only get better. “Full credit to Wirtz on the pitch and the gym, putting in the work,” the Dutchman said. “If someone is struggling physically at the start, you have to keep them in to improve and he was an example of that.” When his pre‑Euro 2024 ranking of potato dishes on the German football federation’s TikTok account went viral (he put a “normal” potato at No 1 to much public amusement), he responded: “I don’t find it entertaining at all.” At the World Cup it still remains to be seen whether he will be given the central role his talent warrants.

data.name: Nathaniel Brown
data.description: “He already ranks among the best full-backs in Europe,” Frankfurt’s sporting director, Markus Krösche, told Bild in April. That may be a bit of an exaggeration but it is true that Brown has had a very, very good season for an otherwise disappointing Eintracht side. Brown is super-quick, strong in his dribbling, a goal threat and – not entirely unimportant for a defender – very hard to get past. With an American father he could have played for the USA, but, after making his international debut in 2025,  it now seems Germany have found a future solution to the problematic left-back position. Or has he already made that his own? “I’m expecting very, very much from him,” Nagelsmann says. “Maybe his time has already come now, but it will definitely come, sooner or later.”

data.name: Leroy Sané
data.description: The general belief in Germany was that Sané had probably missed his chance of making the World Cup squad by moving to Galatasaray in 2025, but here he is. He has not been outstanding in Turkey but staked his claim with two goals and an assist in the crucial World Cup qualifier against Slovakia. And, in truth, he possesses everything a world-class player needs, apart from maybe his weaker foot (the right one). Has been criticised for not making the most of his career but still has stockpiled two Premier League titles and four Bundesliga shields. Not too bad. Early on in his career he had a large tattoo of himself inked on his back.  “Today I wouldn’t have done it,” he admitted to Der Spiegel.

data.name: Nadiem Amiri
data.description: Amiri’s selection was a bit of a surprise. His international career began under Joachim Löw, but he was out of the picture for a long time afterwards, only returning to the fold last year. Julian Nagelsmann still knows Amiri from their Hoffenheim days, when he helped the then 16-year-old begin his professional career, and has praised his “exceptional finishing” and hunger. After picking up a heel injury playing for Mainz, the midfielder missed the March friendlies. In the Bundesliga and in Europe, though, his numbers showed why Nagelsmann could not do without him: 17 goals and three assists, with hardly any other German midfielder posting figures similar. He also made a decisive contribution to World Cup qualification with his first Germany goal against Northern Ireland last September. His cousin Zubayr has over 30 caps for Afghanistan.

data.name: Alexander Nübel
data.description: Nübel has been with Bayern Munich for six years now, joining to be ready when Manuel Neuer retired. And since then has waited, and waited, and waited. There have been loan spells, to Monaco and long-term to Stuttgart but it is debatable whether he will ever be Bayern’s No 1. Nübel is outstanding in one-on-one situations and shots from close range but sometimes struggles with efforts from further out. This is presumably why the national-team coach, Julian Nagelsmann, made Baumann his No 1, with perhaps fewer highs but also fewer lows. Nagelsmann has praised Nübel for being supportive of Baumann. In other words: a good second-choice goalkeeper. But he is now third with Neuer's return.

data.name: David Raum
data.description: There is one title Raum definitely holds: that of the most tattooed German international. His chest reads “Living the dream” – because he gets to be a professional footballer. At one point he had almost given up on that dream, as he recounted in a YouTube video published by his club RB Leipzig. Because he was not always playing at Greuther Fürth, he thought: “OK, maybe football isn’t going to work out after all.” But his career took off after he joined Hoffenheim on a free in 2021 before RB Leipzig paid €26m for him a year later. He started every game for Germany at the World Cup in Qatar, then lost his place, then won it back. Julian Nagelsmann values him for his fighting spirit, among other things. Raum’s strengths are in attack as he is capable of whipping in cross after cross, though a fair few of them end up missing their targets. After his career he wants nothing more to do with football. Instead, he could imagine working as a DJ.

data.name: Felix Nmecha
data.description: Born in Hamburg, raised in England, and developed at Manchester City, that is Nmecha’s backstory. But despite that, and English being his first language, the German FA secured his international future early, along with that of his brother, Lukas. Felix recently extended his contract at Dortmund and is expected to be one of the pillars of the club’s future. He has developed into one of the most physically dominant central midfielders in the league, combining that with good technique and a good shot. Nmecha is happy to talk about his religion and has said: “Jesus is my everything.” Three years ago he liked and shared transphobic and homophobic content on social media, which led some Dortmund fans protesting about him joining the club. Nmecha claimed: “Some things had been taking out of context,” and added: "l'm a Christian and I love all people. I also do not discriminate. I hope all the fans will give me a chance to get to know me and hopefully see that I am a great person."

data.name: Malick Thiaw
data.description: Thiaw can do something that almost certainly no other German international can: speak Finnish. His mother is Finnish, while his father comes from Senegal. It was his father who taught him: “We should be grateful for everything we have.” He moved to Milan at the age of 21 and it was there – no surprise – that he developed an interest in fashion. Mystifying. What’s wrong with three-quarter-length trousers and sandals? Joined Newcastle for €35m last year and immediately established himself as a regular. A quiet leader. Newcastle’s manager, Eddie Howe, describes him as “very, very calm, pretty quiet, but very, very focused”. According to his teammate Nick Woltemade, he can’t head the ball but somehow nodded home two goals in one Premier League game this season.

data.name: Assan Ouédraogo
data.description: The replacement for Lennart Karl, who had to fly back to Germany because of a torn thigh muscle. It came as something of a surprise that Julian Nagelsmann called up Ouédraogo rather than Said El Mala. He has long been regarded as one of Germany’s brightest attacking talents – the youngster from Ruhrgebiet made his debut for Schalke at the age of 17. However, for RB Leipzig, whom Ouédraogo joined in the summer of 2024, he has made only 25 appearances in two years. His problem: he is constantly sidelined by injuries. But when he is fit, the skilled dribbler produces moments of brilliance. He needed just two minutes to score his first international goal. His appearance against Slovakia in November remains his only cap to date. Although Nagelsmann did not originally include him in the squad, it is entirely possible that Ouédraogo will not just be on holiday in the US, but will also get important minutes on the pitch.

data.name: Deniz Undav
data.description: Undav was by far the most clinical German striker this season but, for some reason, Nagelsmann does not seem to rate him. He scored the winning goal in the March friendly against Ghana but afterwards the coach offered no praise, rather a critical backhand. “He wasn’t very involved in the game, had no actions before [the goal],” Nagelsmann said. “I didn’t think his performance was good.” The coach’s comments may have stemmed from Undav saying before the game that he felt he should start rather than just being a substitute, a task Nagelsmann had assigned him in the now infamous “role talks”. Nagelsmann was not entirely wrong, but saying it publicly was unusual. Someone has to put the ball in the net and, in a team without many natural finishers, Undav – a modern-day Gerd Müller – is an exception.
